task.title: Set up markdown-validator from scratch
task.shortdesc: This scenario walks you through the complete setup of markdown-validator — from installation to a running CI gate — in a single guided sequence. Follow it top to bottom the first time you set up the tool for a documentation project.

othermeta.name: description
othermeta.content: A complete end-to-end walkthrough: install markdown-validator, download NLTK data, validate your first file, interpret the report, and add the tool to your GitHub Actions pipeline.

prereq.p: Before you begin, confirm you have the following:

ul.li: Python 3.12 or later installed on your computer. To check: python --version
A documentation repository with at least one .md file to validate.
The path to your team's rule set file. If you do not have one, ask your content lead. The file is typically at rules/tutorial.json in your repository.
Write access to the repository's .github/workflows/ directory (for the CI step).

context.p: Stages: Install → Download language data → Validate a file → Fix failures → Add CI. Each stage links to a detailed task for more guidance.

step.cmd: Create and activate a Python virtual environment.

info.p: Your prompt changes to show (.venv) when the environment is active.

step.stepresult: pip installs markdown-validator and its dependencies. Verify with: md-validate --version

step.cmd: Download the NLTK language data required by sentence-count and part-of-speech rules.

info.note: If your team's rule set does not use sentence-count (s) or part-of-speech (p<N>) rules, you can skip this step. Ask your content lead if you are unsure. For full details, see Download NLTK language data.

step.stepresult: NLTK downloads the corpora to your home directory (~/nltk_data on macOS/Linux). You only need to do this once per Python environment.

step.cmd: Run your first validation against a Markdown file.

info.p: Replace docs/my-article.md with the path to one of your files, and rules/tutorial.json with the path your content lead provided.

step.stepresult: The tool prints a validation report. A fully passing report ends with: Score: 8/8 PASSED A report with failures ends with: Score: 6/8 FAILED

step.cmd: Read the report and identify Required failures.

info.p: Each failed rule shows a cross (✗), the rule name, and a mitigation message:
info.codeblock: ✗ [4] description-must-exist [Required] → Add a description field to the front matter.
info.p: Required failures will block your pull request. Suggested failures are recommendations — they do not block the merge. For a full guide, see Read and act on a validation report.

step.cmd: Open your Markdown file and fix each Required failure using its mitigation message.

ul.li: Missing field — add the key and value to the --- block at the top of the file
Wrong value — change the field value to match what the mitigation message specifies
Stale date — update ms.date to today in YYYY-MM-DD format
Missing H1 — ensure exactly one line begins with a single #
H1 format — edit the H1 to match the prefix in the mitigation message

step.cmd: Re-run validation to confirm all Required failures are resolved.
step.stepresult: The report shows PASSED when all Required rules pass.

step.cmd: Create the GitHub Actions workflow directory if it does not exist.
Create .github/workflows/validate-docs.yml using the complete workflow template in Add markdown-validator to a GitHub Actions workflow.

info.p: The workflow template in that topic is the authoritative version. Copy it verbatim, then update the two values shown below to match your repository:

ul.li: paths: 'docs/**/*.md' — the glob pattern for your documentation files
--rules rules/tutorial.json — the path to your team's rule set file

info.p: The workflow installs markdown-validator, caches and downloads NLTK data, validates the docs directory, and uploads per-file JSON reports as a downloadable artifact — even when validation fails.

step.cmd: Commit the workflow file and push.

info.codeblock: git add .github/workflows/validate-docs.yml git commit -m "Add documentation validation workflow" git push

step.stepresult: GitHub runs the workflow on every subsequent pull request that changes .md files matching the path filter. Required failures block the merge.

step.cmd: Open a test pull request to verify the workflow runs correctly.

info.p: Navigate to the Actions tab of your repository and confirm the Validate documentation check appears and completes. If it fails on a file that passed locally, check that CI is using the same rule set path you tested with.

postreq.p: Share the rule set path, the install steps (1–4), and Read and act on a validation report with your team. To validate the full repository, see Validate a directory.
